questions.question_text: Which of the following best distinguishes between the trait and type approaches to the study of personality in sport psychology and general psychology?

options.text: Trait approach uses continuous dimensions; type approach uses discrete categories
Both approaches use the same discrete categories exclusively
Type approach uses continuous scores; trait approach uses categories
Neither approach is used in modern sport psychology today

questions.explanation: The trait approach describes personality along continuous dimensions such as high-to-low extraversion, while the type approach places people into discrete categories such as introvert, extrovert or endomorph, mesomorph, ectomorph; both have a long history in personality psychology.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The two approaches are not identical in their structure.
The directions are reversed in option C's description.
Both approaches are still used in modern sport psychology.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the basic difference between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation for athletes participating in competitive sport activities?

options.text: Intrinsic comes from within the individual; extrinsic comes from outside rewards
Both intrinsic and extrinsic motivations have the same source
Intrinsic is driven only by external monetary rewards
Extrinsic is driven only by internal feelings of joy

questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from within the individual, such as enjoyment, interest and the satisfaction of mastering a skill; extrinsic motivation comes from outside sources such as money, trophies, social approval or avoiding punishment in sports environments.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The two types of motivation have distinctly different sources.
Monetary rewards are the hallmark of extrinsic motivation.
Internal joy is the hallmark of intrinsic motivation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following describes an athlete who shows a clear mixture of introverted and extroverted features depending on the situation being faced during a sports season?

options.text: A strict introvert with no outward behaviour
A strict extrovert who is loud at all times
An ambivert who adapts across social situations
A person with no recognisable personality type

questions.explanation: An ambivert is an athlete who shows a clear mixture of introverted and extroverted features depending on the situation; they can be talkative in team meetings yet quiet during individual preparation, offering flexibility in many team and individual sport roles in a season.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: A strict introvert is consistently inward-focused.
A strict extrovert is consistently outgoing and loud.
Every healthy person has a recognisable personality pattern.

questions.question_text: Which of the following techniques is most likely to be used by a sport psychologist to help an athlete improve motivation during a long and demanding training season?

options.text: Prescribing only physical exercises without any psychological guidance
Teaching goal-setting, self-talk and positive imagery techniques
Using only intimidation and threats during every practice session
Removing all social support from the athlete's immediate circle

questions.explanation: Sport psychologists often teach goal-setting, constructive self-talk and positive imagery to build motivation; these techniques help athletes clarify meaningful goals, maintain confidence and visualise success, which together support sustained effort during long and demanding training seasons.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Ignoring psychological guidance misses a key motivation tool.
Intimidation and threats tend to damage long-term motivation.
Removing social support can worsen motivation and wellbeing.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most accurate description of the trait 'conscientiousness' in the Big Five model as it applies to athletes and their daily training habits?

options.text: A tendency to be disorganised and forget commitments often
A tendency to be organised, disciplined and responsible in training
A tendency to be creative and experimental in every situation
A tendency to show extreme emotional instability daily

questions.explanation: Conscientiousness in the Big Five model describes a tendency to be organised, disciplined and responsible; in athletes it supports consistent training attendance, careful preparation and reliable execution of plans, all of which tend to support steady long-term performance gains.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Disorganisation describes low, not high, conscientiousness.
Creativity is more closely linked to openness to experience.
Emotional instability refers to neuroticism.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the Big Five trait 'neuroticism' and its typical behavioural manifestation in athletes during high-pressure competitive situations?

options.text: Tendency to feel anxious, tense and negatively affected under pressure
Tendency to be fully calm and composed in every situation always
Tendency to be very sociable and outgoing in team settings
Tendency to be extremely disciplined and organised in training

questions.explanation: Neuroticism refers to a general tendency toward emotional instability and negative affect; athletes high on neuroticism are more likely to feel anxious, tense and negatively affected under pressure and may benefit from specific mental skills training to manage the pressure.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Complete calm at all times is the opposite of neuroticism.
Sociability is captured by extraversion, not neuroticism.
Discipline describes conscientiousness, not neuroticism.

questions.question_text: Which of the following everyday scenarios in sport most clearly illustrates extrinsic motivation operating in a school-aged athlete during the selection phase of a team?

options.text: Practising volleyball daily because it is genuinely enjoyable
Training regularly only to win a school sports award with prize
Playing cricket with friends just for fun after classes
Jogging alone in the park solely for personal health

questions.explanation: Training regularly mainly to win a school sports award and prize illustrates extrinsic motivation, since the drive to participate comes from an external reward rather than from the enjoyment or personal meaning of the activity itself for that school-aged athlete.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Practising because you enjoy the game is intrinsic motivation.
Playing for fun with friends is intrinsic motivation.
Jogging solely for personal health is intrinsic motivation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following approaches to setting goals for a young athlete is most consistent with the widely recommended SMART framework for goal setting in sport psychology?

options.text: A goal that is Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound
A very vague and open-ended goal with no deadline
A goal that is impossibly difficult and unrelated to the sport
A goal that is set once but then completely forgotten

questions.explanation: The SMART framework suggests setting goals that are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound; such goals give athletes clear direction, a way to track progress, realistic challenge and alignment with their sport, all of which support sustained motivation.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Vague, open-ended goals contradict the SMART framework.
Impossibly difficult goals are not achievable or relevant.
Forgotten goals cannot provide motivational direction.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the most appropriate coaching approach for a highly neurotic athlete who becomes very anxious during important competitions and matches?

options.text: Ignore the anxiety and push the athlete harder than others
Teach relaxation, breathing and positive self-talk skills
Publicly criticise the athlete for appearing nervous every time
Ban the athlete from all future competitions permanently

questions.explanation: For a highly neurotic athlete, teaching relaxation skills, controlled breathing and positive self-talk helps regulate emotional arousal and maintain focus during competition; these mental-skills techniques support better performance and wellbeing rather than harsher treatment.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Ignoring anxiety worsens both performance and wellbeing.
Public criticism typically raises anxiety and damages confidence.
Banning an athlete unnecessarily harms their development.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate reason that mental skills training, such as imagery and self-talk, is often included in athletes' overall preparation beyond physical training alone?

options.text: Mental skills training substitutes for all physical training needs
Mental skills training complements physical training and improves performance and coping
Mental skills training is only useful during the off-season period
Mental skills training is unrelated to sport performance outcomes

questions.explanation: Mental skills training such as imagery, self-talk and goal-setting complements physical training; it helps athletes focus attention, manage pressure, sustain motivation and cope with setbacks, all of which contribute to better performance and wellbeing over the long term.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Mental skills cannot substitute for physical training.
These skills are useful in-season as well as off-season.
Mental skills are clearly related to sport performance.

questions.question_text: Match each motivation-related concept in Column A with the most appropriate description listed in Column B and choose the option showing the right combination.

column_b.text: Driven by inner interest and enjoyment
Driven by external rewards or consequences
Constructive inner dialogue used during performance

questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation is driven by inner interest and enjoyment; extrinsic motivation by external rewards or consequences; SMART goals are specific, measurable, achievable, relevant and time-bound; and positive self-talk is a constructive inner dialogue used during performance.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Intrinsic motivation is defined by inner interest and enjoyment.
SMART is a well-known mnemonic for a goal-setting framework.
Self-talk is about constructive inner dialogue during performance.

questions.question_text: Match each Big Five trait in Column A with the typical sport-context description listed in Column B and choose the option that best shows the right combination of the two columns.

column_b.text: Cooperates well with teammates and coaches
Willing to try new tactics and methods of training
Attends training regularly and arrives on time
Enjoys interacting with teammates and fans alike

questions.explanation: Openness is reflected in willingness to try new tactics and training methods; agreeableness in cooperating well with teammates and coaches; extraversion in enjoying interaction with teammates and fans; and conscientiousness in attending training regularly and arriving on time.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Trying new tactics and methods best fits openness.
Cooperating with others matches agreeableness.
Attending training and punctuality match conscientiousness.

questions.question_text: Match each personality classification or theorist in Column A with the corresponding key idea or classification from Column B and choose the option showing the right combination.

column_b.text: OCEAN traits as continuous dimensions of personality

questions.explanation: Sheldon is linked to endomorph, mesomorph and ectomorph somatotypes; Jung to introvert, extrovert and ambivert classification; the Big Five model to OCEAN traits as continuous dimensions; and the trait-versus-type distinction to continuous dimensions versus discrete categories.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Sheldon's classic contribution is the somatotype classification.
Jung is best known for the introvert-extrovert-ambivert categories.
Big Five theory uses OCEAN dimensions as continuous traits.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about the use of SMART goals with athletes and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in motivation practice.
questions.assertion: SMART goals are widely recommended in sport psychology for improving athlete motivation and training adherence.
questions.reason: SMART goals are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound, which gives athletes clear direction and trackable progress.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Clear direction, trackable progress and realistic challenge all support the experience of competence and progress, which feeds motivation and adherence; the SMART criteria deliver these properties directly, so the reason correctly explains why SMART goals are widely recommended.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason correctly explains the benefits of SMART goals.
Both statements align with goal-setting research.
SMART characteristics genuinely support motivation.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about mental imagery in sport and choose the option that best describes their logical connection in sport psychology practice.
questions.assertion: Mental imagery practice, such as vividly visualising successful performance, can improve actual performance in many sports.
questions.reason: Vivid imagery activates neural patterns similar to those used during actual execution and supports skill rehearsal and confidence building.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Vivid imagery activates neural patterns similar to those used during actual execution, supports skill rehearsal and builds confidence; these mechanisms explain why structured mental imagery practice can improve actual performance, directly supporting the assertion in sport psychology research.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason correctly describes the neural mechanism.
Both statements align with sport psychology research.
Imagery practice genuinely supports skill rehearsal.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason regarding personality traits and coaching style and choose the option that best describes their logical connection in youth sport settings.
questions.assertion: A youth sport coach should consider differences in athletes' personality traits when choosing the tone and style of feedback used.
questions.reason: Different athletes respond differently to the same feedback depending on traits such as neuroticism, conscientiousness and agreeableness.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Research shows that athletes differing in neuroticism, conscientiousness and agreeableness respond differently to the same feedback; coaches who adapt tone and style to these differences build trust and support better motivation, which directly explains the recommended approach in the assertion.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason correctly links traits to feedback response.
Both statements align with current coaching literature.
Adapting feedback is genuinely a recommended practice.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following steps of applying the SMART goal-setting framework with an athlete in the correct chronological order as typically recommended in sport psychology practice.

items.text: Define a specific performance outcome to target
Write clear measurable criteria for progress
Set a realistic but challenging time-bound deadline
Review progress periodically and adjust if needed

options.text: P → Q → R → S
Q → P → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → Q → S

questions.explanation: A SMART goal is built by first defining a specific performance outcome; then writing clear measurable criteria for progress; then setting a realistic but challenging time-bound deadline; and finally reviewing progress periodically and adjusting if needed over time.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Defining the specific target must come before measuring it.
Measurable criteria precede setting a clear deadline.
Review and adjustment form the closing step of the cycle.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following steps of a structured mental imagery session for an athlete in the correct chronological order as typically described by sport psychologists.

items.text: Relax the body with controlled breathing exercises
Vividly visualise successful performance of the skill
Review the session and reflect on clarity and emotion
Set a clear purpose for the imagery practice

options.text: S → P → Q → R
P → Q → R → S
R → Q → P → S
Q → P → S → R

questions.explanation: A structured imagery session begins with setting a clear purpose for the practice; then relaxing the body with controlled breathing; then vividly visualising successful performance of the skill; and finally reviewing the session and reflecting on clarity and emotion afterward.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Setting purpose precedes any relaxation in imagery sessions.
Relaxation precedes active visualisation.
Review and reflection form the closing step of an imagery session.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: In sport psychology, personality is generally considered a stable pattern of traits that can still be shaped by learning and experience over time.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Personality in sport psychology is generally considered a relatively stable pattern of traits that remains consistent across situations and time; however, it can still be shaped by learning, coaching, experience and life circumstances over the longer term of an athlete's career.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Personality is generally stable across situations.
Learning and experience can still shape personality.
These two ideas are compatible in modern research.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram summarising major motivation concepts and techniques in sport psychology; identify the motivational technique most closely associated with the acronym SMART in goal-setting practice with athletes.

options.text: Positive self-talk practice
SMART goal-setting framework
Progressive muscle relaxation method
Positive mental imagery rehearsal

questions.explanation: SMART is an acronym used in the goal-setting framework, standing for Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound; it is widely used with athletes to design structured goals that support clear direction, measurable progress and sustained motivation over time.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Positive self-talk is a separate mental-skills technique.
Relaxation methods are separate tools for arousal regulation.
Mental imagery rehearsal is a distinct visualisation technique.
